title: Versal letters: large decorated initials
subtitle: Built-up broad-edge capital letters in the medieval manuscript tradition
excerpt: A versal is a large capital letter built from multiple pen strokes rather than a single drawn stroke. Versals are the decorated initial letters of medieval manuscripts: they stand 5 to 10 lines tall, are outlined and filled in two passes, and are optionally gilded or painted.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on versals and built-up letters. The versal analysis and construction method described here follows Johnston's canonical approach to historical manuscript versals. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

content.text: The
versal
is a
built-up

capital: made by drawing the left edge of each stroke with one pen pass, the right edge with another, and then filling in the space between. The resulting letter is much larger than a single pen-stroke capital at the same nib width; it can stand 5 to 10 text lines tall without the proportions becoming awkward. Johnston's analysis in Writing and Illuminating and Lettering remains the canonical method.
What you need

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, No. 0 (the broadest) for large versals; No. 3 for smaller versions

items.qty: Small amount, mixed to a creamy consistency
items.name: Calligraphy gouache in a rich colour (red, blue, or black for traditional work)
Tracing paper (for planning the letterform before committing to ink)

content.text: The construction principle

Every stroke in a versal is a thin hairline that sets one side of the stroke. A matching hairline sets the other side. You fill the space in between. For a vertical stem, the first stroke outlines the left edge from top to bottom. The second stroke outlines the right edge, running parallel and leaving a gap of 3 to 4 nib widths. To fill, hold the nib at 90 degrees and flood colour between the two outlines.
Ruling the letter

Rule two horizontal lines for the cap height: baseline and cap-height line. For a versal standing 5 text lines tall, the cap-height line is 5 times the x-height of the accompanying text above the baseline.

Lightly sketch the letterform in pencil to plan the proportions. For the classical Roman-based versal, the letter O is a near-circle; the letter I has serifs; the letter H has stems of equal weight. Erase the sketch after inking if pencil shows.
Building the letter: the stem example

Hold the nib at 90 degrees to the baseline (straight up and down). This gives a hairline on both sides of the stroke. Draw the left edge of the stem from the top serif to the base serif in a single smooth downstroke. The hairline produced is the left edge of the versal stroke.
Draw the right edge 3 to 4 nib widths to the right of the first stroke, again from top to bottom. The two strokes together now define the full width of the stem.

Fill in the space between the two outlines by making short horizontal strokes across the gap, working from top to bottom. Do not drag the fill; make each small stroke separately. The fill should be dense and even, with no holidays (gaps in the fill).

Add the serifs: each serif is a short hairline stroke crossing the top and bottom of the stem at right angles, then filled in the same way as the stem. The classical versal serif is thin and horizontal.
Curved strokes and the O

For the O: the nib angle changes around the curve. At the thickest point (left side of the letter, roughly 9 o'clock), the nib is at 90 degrees and the stroke is widest. At the thinnest point (top and bottom of the letter, 12 and 6 o'clock), the nib rotates to 0 degrees so the stroke becomes a hairline. This modulated nib angle, rotating continuously as the curve proceeds, is what gives the classical O its thick-and-thin rhythm. Build the O in two half-circles: left half (the shade side) first, then the right half.
Care and finishing

Allow the versal to dry completely before erasing any pencil guide lines underneath. Erasing over wet gouache smears the colour. A finished versal can be left in colour, outlined in a darker shade with a fine nib, or gilded on a gesso ground if following the full illuminated manuscript tradition.
